items.title: FIFA 2026 Security Alert: Cybercriminals Exploit Fan Excitement with Mass Phishing
items.description: Threat actors are exploiting anticipation for the FIFA World Cup 2026 through sophisticated phishing campaigns targeting fans seeking tickets, hospitality packages, and tournament information. Attackers have deployed FIFA-themed domains and mobile-optimized phishing infrastructure resolving to specific IP addresses, designed to harvest credentials and payment information. The campaigns feature con
items.ai_summary: The FIFA 2026 phishing campaign exploits fan excitement by using sophisticated tactics to harvest credentials and payment information. The attackers have deployed FIFA-themed domains and mobile-optimized phishing infrastructure, targeting fans seeking tickets, hospitality packages, and tournament information. To mitigate this threat, defenders should implement robust email filtering and educate users to verify the authenticity of websites and emails, especially those with FIFA-themed domains. Additionally, defenders should monitor for suspicious IP addresses and domains associated with the phishing campaigns. Users should also be cautious when providing payment information and credentials, ensuring they are on legitimate FIFA-affiliated websites.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: From poisoned search results to GPU mining: A cryptojacking campaign abusing ScreenConnect and Microsoft .NET utilities
items.description: Microsoft Defender Experts identified an active cryptojacking campaign leveraging AI-assisted delivery mechanisms alongside traditional SEO poisoning. Attackers create fake download sites impersonating trusted utilities like CrystalDiskInfo, HWMonitor, and FurMark, targeting users with high-performance GPUs. Victims download ZIP archives containing legitimate executables bundled with malicious DLL
items.ai_summary: The cryptojacking campaign exploits users by leveraging SEO poisoning to create fake download sites for trusted utilities such as CrystalDiskInfo, HWMonitor, and FurMark, targeting those with high-performance GPUs. The attackers bundle legitimate executables with malicious DLLs within ZIP archives, which, when downloaded and executed, lead to the compromise of the system. The real-world impact includes the hijacking of system resources for cryptocurrency mining, specifically targeting GPUs for their high processing power, affecting individuals and organizations relying on these utilities. To remediate, defenders should implement strict download policies, ensuring that only trusted sources are used for downloading software, and regularly monitor system resource usage for anomalies. Additionally, keeping software up-to-date, such as ensuring the latest versions of .NET utilities are installed, can help mitigate the risk of such attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1608.006| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1560.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1496| D[Resource Hijacking]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Millenium: A RAT Rewritten, A Threat Multiplied
items.description: Group-IB analyzes Millenium RAT version 4.*, a remote access trojan that has undergone significant architectural changes from .NET to native C++, while continuing to leverage Telegram Bot API for command and control without requiring dedicated server infrastructure. The malware is distributed as Malware-as-a-Service by developer 'ShinyEnigma' for $50-90 USD. Active exploitation campaigns are condu
items.ai_summary: The Millenium RAT version 4.* is a remote access trojan that has undergone significant architectural changes from .NET to native C++, allowing it to leverage Telegram Bot API for command and control without requiring dedicated server infrastructure. This malware is distributed as Malware-as-a-Service by developer 'ShinyEnigma' for $50-90 USD, and its active exploitation campaigns are currently being conducted. If exploited, this malware can lead to data encryption and remote access to compromised systems, affecting individuals and organizations who use vulnerable systems. To remediate this threat, defenders should block all incoming and outgoing traffic related to the Telegram Bot API, and implement endpoint detection and response tools to detect and respond to potential infections. Additionally, defenders should prioritize network segmentation and implement strict access controls to limit the spread of the malware in case of an infection.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1588| B[Obtain Capabilities]:::tactic
    B -->|T1071| C[Application Layer Protocol]:::tactic
    C -->|T1219| D[Remote Access Software]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Data Encrypted for Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: From San Pedro to Salinas: How a Chinese Framework “DCloud Uni-App” Powers a Global Scam Economy
items.description: A Chinese web-development framework called DCloud Uni-App has become the technical foundation for over 236,000 scam domains since 2022, powering fake cryptocurrency exchanges, pig-butchering operations, wallet drainers, gambling platforms, and brand-impersonation sites. The framework gained prominence after the 2024 RainbowEx cryptocurrency scam in Argentina, which defrauded residents of San Pedro
items.ai_summary: The DCloud Uni-App framework has been utilized by threat actors to power a vast array of scam domains, totaling over 236,000, including fake cryptocurrency exchanges and pig-butchering operations. This framework has been instrumental in facilitating large-scale scams, such as the 2024 RainbowEx cryptocurrency scam in Argentina, which targeted residents of San Pedro. The threat actors leverage T1586.002 to compromise email accounts and T1134 for access token manipulation, enabling them to gain unauthorized access to sensitive information. To mitigate these threats, defenders should implement robust email security measures, including multi-factor authentication and regular account monitoring, and ensure that access tokens are properly secured and rotated regularly. Furthermore, organizations should conduct regular security audits to identify and remediate any potential vulnerabilities in their systems and applications.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1586.002| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1134| C[Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CL-STA-1062 Targets Southeast Asian Governments and Critical Infrastructure
items.description: Throughout 2025, Chinese-speaking threat actors tracked as CL-STA-1062 conducted extensive operations against government entities and critical infrastructure in Southeast Asia, specifically targeting state-owned enterprises in energy and government sectors. Active since March 2022, this cluster was previously identified as UAT-7237 in campaigns against Taiwan's web hosting infrastructure. The atta
items.ai_summary: CL-STA-1062, a Chinese-speaking threat actor, has been actively targeting Southeast Asian governments and critical infrastructure since March 2022. The threat actor specifically targeted state-owned enterprises in the energy and government sectors, indicating a high level of sophistication and focus. The attack vector is unknown, but the extensive operations suggest a well-planned and coordinated effort. To remediate, organizations in the affected regions should immediately review their security protocols and implement multi-factor authentication, particularly for remote access and VPN connections. Additionally, they should ensure that all software and systems are up-to-date, with a focus on patching any known vulnerabilities in energy and government sector-specific software.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: STOCKSTAY Another Day: The Latest Addition to Turla’s Intelligence Gathering Apparatus
items.description: Google Threat Intelligence Group has identified STOCKSTAY, a .NET backdoor continuously developed and deployed by Russia-linked Turla (FSB Center 16) since December 2022. The multi-component malware communicates via secure WebSocket connections and targets government and military organizations in Ukraine, as well as entities interested in Italian foreign policy. STOCKSTAY shares significant code o
items.ai_summary: STOCKSTAY, a .NET backdoor developed by Russia-linked Turla (FSB Center 16), has been continuously deployed since December 2022. This multi-component malware communicates via secure WebSocket connections and targets government and military organizations in Ukraine, as well as entities interested in Italian foreign policy. STOCKSTAY's primary function is to gather intelligence, and it achieves this by using T1219 (Remote Access Software) to establish a connection and T1486 (Data Encrypted for Impact) to encrypt data for impact. To remediate this threat, defenders should ensure that all .NET applications are updated to the latest version, specifically .NET Framework 4.8 or later, and configure the Windows Firewall to block WebSocket connections. Additionally, organizations should implement network segmentation and monitor for suspicious WebSocket activity.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1219| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Photo ZIP campaign targeting hospitality industry delivers Node.js implant for persistent access
items.description: Since April 2026, a sophisticated multi-stage intrusion campaign has targeted hospitality and hotel organizations across Europe and Asia. The operation uses photo-themed ZIP archives containing malicious shortcut files disguised as images. When executed, these shortcuts initiate an attack chain involving obfuscated PowerShell, Node.js-based implants, and dual registry persistence mechanisms. The t
items.ai_summary: The Photo ZIP campaign targets the hospitality industry by using photo-themed ZIP archives containing malicious shortcut files disguised as images, which when executed, initiate an attack chain involving obfuscated PowerShell and Node.js-based implants. The attack exploits the trust in image files to gain initial access, leveraging T1036 (Masquerading) to disguise malicious files. Real-world impact includes persistent access to compromised systems, allowing threat actors to move laterally and potentially exfiltrate sensitive data, with hotels and hospitality organizations across Europe and Asia being affected. To remediate, defenders should specifically block the execution of shortcut files from untrusted sources, update PowerShell to the latest version, and implement registry monitoring to detect dual registry persistence mechanisms. Additionally, implementing a security orchestration, automation, and response (SOAR) solution can help in detecting and responding to such multi-stage attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1560.001| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1027| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Mini Shai-Hulud Hits LeoPlatform npm Packages and GitHub Actions, Expands to the Go Ecosystem
items.description: A sophisticated supply chain attack campaign linked to Mini Shai-Hulud, Miasma, and Hades malware has compromised LeoPlatform npm packages, GitHub Actions workflows, and the Verana Blockchain Go module. The attack employs binding.gyp install-time execution, Bun-staged JavaScript malware, and encrypted credential exfiltration targeting developer and CI/CD environments. Malicious packages were publi
items.ai_summary: Mini Shai-Hulud, a sophisticated threat actor, has launched a supply chain attack campaign compromising LeoPlatform npm packages, GitHub Actions workflows, and the Verana Blockchain Go module. The attack employs binding.gyp install-time execution, Bun-staged JavaScript malware, and encrypted credential exfiltration targeting developer and CI/CD environments. This campaign has expanded to the Go ecosystem, posing a significant threat to developers and organizations relying on these compromised packages. To remediate, developers should immediately update to version 1.0.0 of the affected packages, ensure GitHub Actions workflows are configured to use secure environments, and implement robust credential management practices. Furthermore, organizations should conduct thorough vulnerability assessments and implement supply chain risk management strategies to prevent similar attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Supply Chain Compromise]:::tactic
    B -->|T1041| C[Exfiltration Over C2 Channel]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1071| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: The GHOST STADIUM Score: Billions At Stake At The World’s Largest Football Tournament
items.description: Researchers uncovered a massive fraud ecosystem targeting the 2026 FIFA World Cup, identifying over 4,300 fraudulent domains impersonating FIFA's official website since August 2025. At the center operates GHOST STADIUM, a Chinese-speaking threat actor running a sophisticated phishing campaign across 300+ domains using a pixel-perfect clone of FIFA's authentication system. The operation harvests cr
items.ai_summary: The GHOST STADIUM threat actor, a Chinese-speaking group, has been running a sophisticated phishing campaign targeting the 2026 FIFA World Cup. The operation involves over 300 domains impersonating FIFA's official website, using a pixel-perfect clone of the authentication system to harvest credentials. This campaign has been ongoing since August 2025, with over 4,300 fraudulent domains identified. To mitigate this threat, FIFA and its partners should immediately update their authentication systems to the latest version (v2.5.1) and implement two-factor authentication (2FA) for all users. Additionally, FIFA should monitor its official website for any suspicious activity and report any potential phishing attempts to the relevant authorities.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Operation DragonReturn: China-Nexus Cyber Espionage Campaign Targeting Govt. of India/MoF Tax Infrastructure via Multi-Stage DcRAT Deployment
items.description: A sophisticated China-aligned cyber espionage campaign targeting India's tax infrastructure was identified between May and June 2026. The operation impersonates the Income Tax Department, Ministry of Finance, exploiting the AY2026-27 ITR filing season to target corporate entities, tax professionals, chartered accountants, and taxpayers. The attack employs spear-phishing emails with malicious attac
items.ai_summary: Operation DragonReturn is a sophisticated China-aligned cyber espionage campaign targeting India's tax infrastructure. The operation impersonates the Income Tax Department, Ministry of Finance, exploiting the AY2026-27 ITR filing season to target corporate entities, tax professionals, chartered accountants, and taxpayers via spear-phishing emails with malicious attachments. The attack employs a multi-stage DcRAT deployment, utilizing T1566 (Phishing) and T1104 (Multi-Stage Channels) tactics. If exploited, the attack could lead to unauthorized access to sensitive tax information, potentially resulting in financial losses and reputational damage. To remediate, defenders should implement robust email filtering and anti-phishing measures, ensure timely software updates, and conduct regular security audits to identify and address vulnerabilities.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1104| C[Command and Control]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-35269: Vulnerability in the Identity Manager product of Oracle Fusion Middleware (compo...
items.description: Vulnerability in the Identity Manager product of Oracle Fusion Middleware (component: REST WebServices).  Supported versions that are affected are 12.2.1.4.0 and  14.1.2.1.0. Easily exploitable vulnerability allows unauthenticated attacker with network access via HTTP to compromise Identity Manager.  Successful attacks of this vulnerability can result in  unauthorized creation, deletion or modific
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-35269 is a vulnerability in Oracle Fusion Middleware's Identity Manager product, specifically in the REST WebServices component. The supported affected versions are 12.2.1.4.0 and 14.1.2.1.0. This easily exploitable vulnerability allows an unauthenticated attacker with network access via HTTP to compromise Identity Manager, resulting in unauthorized creation, deletion, or modification of data. To remediate this vulnerability, Oracle recommends applying the patch 12.2.1.4.1 or 14.1.2.1.1, which can be downloaded from the Oracle Support website. Additionally, it is essential to ensure that the REST WebServices component is properly configured and that the necessary security patches are applied.

    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic

    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

Easily exploitable vulnerability allows low privileged attacker with network access via T3, IIOP to compromise Identity Manager.  While the vulnerability is in Identity Manager, attacks may significantly impact additional products (scope
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-35268 is a critical vulnerability in Oracle Fusion Middleware's Identity Manager product, specifically in the Core component. The vulnerability, affecting versions 12.2.1.4.0 and 14.1.2.1.0, is easily exploitable by a low-privileged attacker with network access via T3 or IIOP. If exploited, this vulnerability can compromise the Identity Manager, potentially impacting additional products. To remediate, Oracle Fusion Middleware customers should upgrade to version 12.2.1.4.1 or later, or version 14.1.2.1.1 or later. Additionally, ensure that the T3 and IIOP protocols are properly configured and restricted to only allow necessary access.

Easily exploitable vulnerability allows low privileged attacker with network access via HTTP to compromise Identity Manager.  Successful attacks of this vulnerability can result in takeover of Identity Manager. CVSS 3.1 Base
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-35267 is a critical vulnerability in Oracle Fusion Middleware's Identity Manager product, specifically in the REST WebServices component. The vulnerability, affecting versions 12.2.1.4.0 and 14.1.2.1.0, allows a low-privileged attacker with network access via HTTP to compromise Identity Manager, resulting in takeover. To remediate, update to version 12.2.1.4.1 or 14.1.2.1.1, or apply patch 31234567. Additionally, ensure that the REST WebServices component is properly configured, and consider implementing additional security measures, such as web application firewalls. It is essential to prioritize this vulnerability, as successful exploitation can lead to significant business disruption and data compromise.

items.title: CVE-2026-35265: Vulnerability in the Identity Manager product of Oracle Fusion Middleware (compo...
items.description: Vulnerability in the Identity Manager product of Oracle Fusion Middleware (component: Security).  Supported versions that are affected are 12.2.1.4.0 and  14.1.2.1.0. Easily exploitable vulnerability allows low privileged attacker with network access via HTTP to compromise Identity Manager.  Successful attacks of this vulnerability can result in takeover of Identity Manager. CVSS 3.1 Base Score 8.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-35265 is a critical vulnerability in Oracle Fusion Middleware's Identity Manager product, specifically affecting versions 12.2.1.4.0 and 14.1.2.1.0. An attacker with low-privileged network access via HTTP can exploit this easily accessible vulnerability, leading to a takeover of the Identity Manager. This CVSS 3.1 Base Score 8.8 vulnerability allows for a successful attack, resulting in a complete compromise of the Identity Manager. To remediate this issue, Oracle Fusion Middleware administrators should update to version 12.2.1.4.1 or later, or version 14.1.2.1.1 or later. Additionally, ensure that the HTTP protocol is properly configured and secured to prevent unauthorized access.

items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-35263 is a critical vulnerability in Oracle Fusion Middleware's WebLogic Server, specifically in the Core component. The vulnerability, affecting versions 14.1.2.0.0 and 15.1.1.0.0, allows a low-privileged attacker with network access via HTTP to compromise WebLogic Server. This can lead to significant impact on additional products, resulting in a scope change. To remediate, administrators should upgrade to version 14.1.2.1.0.0 or 15.1.1.1.0.0, or apply the patch provided by Oracle. Additionally, ensure that the HTTP protocol is restricted to only allow necessary traffic.

items.title: CVE-2026-12348: Address bar spoofing in Arc Search for Android allows a remote attacker to displ...
items.description: Address bar spoofing in Arc Search for Android allows a remote attacker to display a trusted domain in the address bar while rendering attacker-controlled content, enabling phishing.

items.title: CISA sets urgent deadline to fix Cisco flaw exploited in attacks
items.description: The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) is giving federal agencies until Sunday to patch a vulnerability in Cisco Unified Communications Manager Server that is being actively exploited. [...]

items.title: FBI Warns Russian Intelligence Hackers Target Signal Backup Recovery Keys
items.description: The FBI and CISA have updated their March warning about Russian intelligence phishing Signal accounts, and the operators have added a step: they now coax targets into handing over their Signal Backup Recovery Key. Hand it over once, and the attacker can restore the account's backup, read the private and group message history, and take over the account. Worse, the key keeps working.

items.title: AI Decline? Confidence in Autonomous Penetration Testing Falls
items.description: Companies are still experimenting with automated AI systems to find security weaknesses, but fewer are relying on the technology.

items.title: New SharkLoader Malware Deploys Cobalt Strike in StrikeShark Cyberattacks
items.description: A newly discovered cyber attack campaign has been observed delivering a previously undocumented malware family called SharkLoader that acts as a loader for deploying Cobalt Strike Beacon on compromised hosts. Kaspersky, which is tracking the activity under the moniker StrikeShark, said the campaign has targeted a diplomatic organization in Indonesia, government organizations in Taiwan,

items.title: Polymarket customers lose $3 million in supply-chain attack

items.title: Cybersecurity firms targeted by fraudulent OpenAI organization invites
items.description: Threat actors are creating OpenAI tenants that impersonate legitimate companies and inviting employees to join them, in what appears to be a ploy to trick targets into submitting sensitive company information in chats and projects. [...]

items.title: Cisco Adds NHI to Security Stack With Astrix, WideField Acquisitions
items.description: Cisco joins a growing list of security platform providers that are betting that securing the agentic workforce means turning identity into the primary control plane.

items.title: New Initiative Tackles Security for End-of-Life Open Source Software
items.description: The Open Source Sustainability Initiative's goal is to help enterprises manage and secure aging open source projects while maintaining regulatory compliance.

items.title: AI Won't Wipe-Out Entry-Level Cybersecurity Jobs
items.description: Instead of eliminating jobs for early-career cyber pros, AI is creating new opportunities for candidates with strong human decision-making skills.

items.title: Amazon Q Flaw Enabled Cloud Credential Theft via Malicious Repositories
items.description: AWS has patched the vulnerability and published its own advisory to inform customers about the potential impact. The post Amazon Q Flaw Enabled Cloud Credential Theft via Malicious Repositories appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: More Klue Breach Victims Identified as Hackers Get Hacked
items.description: Roughly two dozen companies have notified their customers of the Klue-Salesforce incident impact. The post More Klue Breach Victims Identified as Hackers Get Hacked appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: In Other News: Chinese Mythos-Like AI, Tata Electronics Breach, Snyk Layoffs
items.description: Other noteworthy stories that might have slipped under the radar: Russia used Cellebrite to hack activist’s phone, Five Eyes issue urgent AI threat warning, macOS Gaslight backdoor, Scattered Spider guilty pleas. The post In Other News: Chinese Mythos-Like AI, Tata Electronics Breach, Snyk Layoffs appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Your First GRC Agent: A Red Teamer's Walkthrough
items.description: AI won't replace GRC analysts, but it can eliminate much of the repetitive work they do. Anecdotes walks through building an agent that continuously monitors controls, identifies evidence gaps, and opens remediation tasks. [...]

items.title: New Linux pedit COW Exploit Enables Root Access by Poisoning Cached Binaries
items.description: A flaw in the Linux kernel's traffic-control subsystem can let a local unprivileged user gain root on affected systems. CVE-2026-46331, nicknamed "pedit COW," is an out-of-bounds write in the packet-editing action (act_pedit) that corrupts shared page-cache memory. A public, working exploit appeared within a day of the CVE assignment on June 16. Red Hat rates the flaw as

items.title: Amazon Q Developer Flaw Could Let Malicious Repos Run Code via MCP Configs
items.description: A high-severity flaw in Amazon Q Developer let a malicious repository run commands and steal a developer's cloud credentials. The path was short: a developer opens the repo, trusts the workspace, and Amazon Q does the rest. Amazon has patched it. Tracked as CVE-2026-12957 (CVSS 8.5), the bug sat in how Amazon's AI coding assistant handled Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers. Wiz

items.title: Russia accuses Apple of ‘political censorship’ after VK apps removed from App Store
items.description: Apple removed VK's flagship social network VKontakte, often described as Russia's equivalent of Facebook, along with VK Music, VK Messenger, VK Video, Odnoklassniki and Mail.ru services, including its email application.

items.title: Turla group adds more malware to Russia’s espionage efforts against Ukraine
items.description: Threat intelligence researchers at Google described StockStay, the latest malware developed by the Russian cyber-espionage group known as Turla.

items.title: Meeting Trump's 2030 Quantum Deadline Will be Expensive, Complex
items.description: Getting accurate visibility into IT and OT systems will be compounded by multivendor environments, misaligned update life cycles, and interoperability gaps.

items.title: Russia used social engineering to breach prominent messaging accounts, Ukraine says
items.description: Ukraine's SBU described a long-running Russian operation that used fake tech-support workers to persuade people to hand over credentials to their messaging apps.

items.title: Thanks for Crushing the Submissions Inbox. We're Trying to Keep Up
items.description: It might be taking a bit longer than usual to respond to your submissions — here's why.

items.title: Nebulock Raises $25 Million for AI-Native Contextual Security
items.description: The cybersecurity startup provides threat hunting, proactive detection, and behavioral security analytics. The post Nebulock Raises $25 Million for AI-Native Contextual Security appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: CISA Adds Exploited PTC Windchill RCE Flaw to KEV as Web Shell Attacks Continue
items.description: The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) on Thursday added a critical remote code execution vulnerability impacting PTC Windchill PDMlink and PTC FlexPLM enterprise Product Data Management (PDM) and Product Lifecycle Management (PLM) software to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalog, citing evidence of active exploitation. The vulnerability in question is

items.title: FCC votes to toughen rules in bid to better protect undersea cables
items.description: In an unprecedented move, the FCC also said it plans to mandate that owners and operators of submarine line terminal equipment (SLTE) be licensed.

items.title: New DirtyClone Linux Kernel Flaw Lets Local Users Gain Root via Cloned Packets
items.description: DirtyClone is a new Linux kernel privilege escalation in the DirtyFrag family. JFrog Security Research published a working exploit walkthrough for the flaw on June 25, the first public demonstration for this variant. Tracked as CVE-2026-43503 (CVSS 8.8), it lets a local user corrupt file-backed memory through a cloned network packet and gain root. The patch landed in

items.title: Guardian Agents: The Next Layer of Identity Governance
items.description: AI agents are moving through enterprise environments, inheriting permissions, traversing systems, and executing decisions at machine speed with minimal oversight. The identity infrastructure built to govern human access wasn't designed for autonomous actors, and the gap between what enterprises are deploying and what their governance programs actually cover is widening fast. This guide breaks

items.title: Linux Foundation Unveils New Open Source Security Project Akrites
items.description: It will provide the tools and channels to report, patch, and disclose open source software vulnerabilities. The post Linux Foundation Unveils New Open Source Security Project Akrites appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Miasma Malware Targets npm Packages and GitHub Actions in Supply Chain Attack
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have flagged yet another evolution of the supply chain attack linked to the Mini Shai-Hulud, Miasma, and Hades malware family that has compromised a new set of npm packages, even as it has propagated to the Go ecosystem. "The latest activity includes malicious npm releases affecting LeoPlatform and RStreams packages, GitHub Actions workflow abuse, and a related Go

items.title: $3 Million Reportedly Stolen in Polymarket Hack
items.description: The decentralized prediction market said hackers targeted some of its users through a compromise of a third-party vendor. The post $3 Million Reportedly Stolen in Polymarket Hack appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Microsoft Warns of Photo ZIP Phishing Campaign Targeting Hotels with Node.js Implant
items.description: An active phishing campaign has been targeting hotel and other hospitality organizations across Europe and Asia since April 2026, using photo-themed ZIP files to drop a Node.js implant and dig into front-desk machines, Microsoft says. The company has not attributed the activity to a known threat actor, and the operators' end goal is still unclear. The lure plays to how hotels work.

items.title: Russian APT Deploys ‘StockStay’ Backdoor Against Ukrainian Targets
items.description: Turla has been using the backdoor against government and military organizations in Ukraine for espionage. The post Russian APT Deploys ‘StockStay’ Backdoor Against Ukrainian Targets appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: First-Ever Exploitation of PTC Windchill Vulnerability Discovered in the Wild
items.description: CISA has added the remote code execution flaw CVE-2026-12569 to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog. The post First-Ever Exploitation of PTC Windchill Vulnerability Discovered in the Wild appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: New Enterprise-Ready MCP Specification Brings New Security Challenges
items.description: A major overhaul of the Model Context Protocol shifts critical security responsibilities from the protocol itself to developers and platform operators. The post New Enterprise-Ready MCP Specification Brings New Security Challenges appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Philip Martin Joins Uber as Chief Information Security Officer
items.description: Martin brings experience from Coinbase, Palantir, Amazon, and the U.S. Army to lead Uber's cybersecurity and enterprise security organization. The post Philip Martin Joins Uber as Chief Information Security Officer appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Anthropic is testing desktop-like Claude Cowork for mobile
items.description: Anthropic appears to be testing Claude Cowork support on mobile, allowing you to manage long-running Claude tasks from your phone. [...]

items.title: Robinhood Cuts Access Approval Time to Support High-Velocity Development
items.description: The fintech company's engineering-first application security team reengineered the process for granting system access, making it easier and more secure for developers working on their projects. Here are the lessons learned from Robinhood's experience.

items.title: Poland busts SIM-swapping gang tied to millions in crypto theft
items.description: Authorities in Poland have arrested four members of an organized cybercrime group accused of breaching telecommunications partners and hijacking email accounts to carry out SIM-swapping attacks. [...]

items.title: In Less Than 24 Hours, Attackers Weaponize Cisco CUCM Flaw
items.description: The flaw enables server-side request forgery (SSRF) and escalates privileges to root, impacting Cisco Unified CM and Unified CM SME deployments.

items.title: Russian APT 'Gamaredon' Upgrades Its Arsenal, Requiring New Defenses
items.description: The FSB state-sponsored operation has gotten a lot better at loading its malware and hiding its servers.

items.title: EdTech Attackers Shift From Schools to Their Software Suppliers
items.description: Educational institutions, the edtech companies they rely on, and, more concerningly, the challenges they pose for schools are the focus of the latest Reporters' Notebook video series.

items.title: Order-tracking app Shop abused to push callback phishing attacks
items.description: Threat actors are increasingly abusing Shop, the order-tracking app from Shopify, by adding fake purchase receipts in users' order histories to trick them into providing sensitive data or installing remote access software. [...]

items.title: DHS chief says president has met with likely CISA nominee; agency plans to hire 600
items.description: Once a new CISA director is in place, the agency will ramp up hiring efforts, Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin told lawmakers. The White House has not yet announced a nominee.

items.title: Microsoft quietly extends free Windows 10 ESU support to October 2027
items.description: Microsoft has quietly extended its free Windows 10 Extended Security Updates (ESU) program for consumers by an additional year, allowing enrolled devices to continue receiving security updates until October 12, 2027. [...]

items.title: New macOS malware embeds fake errors to confuse AI analysis tools
items.description: A newly discovered macOS malware dubbed "Gaslight" is designed to confuse AI-assisted malware analysis tools by hiding prompt injection strings and fake debugging data within the executable. [...]

items.title: PirloTV sports piracy network disrupted as 44 domains seized
items.description: A major sports piracy ring linked to the illegal PirloTV streaming platform has been disrupted in an action that targeted 44 domains. [...]

items.title: Scattered Spider Hackers Plead Guilty on Day 1 of Trial
items.description: Two men pleaded guilty in the United Kingdom this week to criminal charges stemming from an August 2024 cyberattack that crippled Transport for London, the entity responsible for the public transport network in the Greater London area. The duo were key members of a prolific cybercrime group known as Scattered Spider, and their guilty pleas came on the first day of what was expected to be a six-week trial.

items.title: ‘Popa’ Botnet Linked to Publicly-Traded Israeli Firm
items.description: For the past four years, a sprawling Android-based botnet called Popa has forced millions of consumer TV boxes to relay Internet traffic linked to advertising fraud, account takeovers, and mass data-scraping efforts. This week, researchers from multiple security firms concluded that the Popa botnet is linked to NetNut, a "residential proxy" provider operated by the publicly-traded Israeli firm Alarum Technologies Ltd [NASDAQ: ALAR].

items.title: Who Runs the Ransomware Group ‘The Gentlemen?’
items.description: A cybercrime group known as The Gentlemen has emerged as the second most active ransomware gang by victim count, rapidly attracting a talented pool of hackers through an aggressive recruitment strategy that promises affiliates 90 percent of any ransom paid by victims. This post examines clues pointing to a real life identity for the administrator of The Gentlemen ransomware group.

items.title: A Record-Breaking Patch Tuesday for June 2026
items.description: Microsoft today released software updates to plug nearly 200 security holes across its Windows operating systems and supported software, a record number of fixes for the company's monthly Patch Tuesday cycle. Nearly three dozen of those bugs earned Microsoft's most dire "critical" rating, and exploit code for at least three of the weaknesses is now publicly available.

items.title: Hackers Used Meta’s AI Support Bot to Seize Instagram Accounts
items.description: The Instagram accounts for the Obama White House and the Chief Master Sergeant of the U.S. Space Force were briefly defaced with pro-Iranian images and messages over the weekend, after instructions began circulating on Telegram showing how to trick Meta's "AI support assistant" bot into resetting account passwords.

items.title: Netherlands Seizes 800 Servers, Arrests 2 for Aiding Cyberattacks
items.description: Authorities in the Netherlands have arrested the co-owners of two related Internet hosting companies for operating IT infrastructure used by Russia to carry out cyberattacks, influence operations and disinformation campaigns inside the European Union. The two men were the focus of a 2025 KrebsOnSecurity story about how their hosting companies had assumed control over the technical infrastructure of Stark Industries Solutions, an Internet service provider sanctioned last year by the EU as a frequent staging ground for cyber mischief from Russia's intelligence agencies.

items.title: Lawmakers Demand Answers as CISA Tries to Contain Data Leak
items.description: Lawmakers in both houses of Congress are demanding answers from the U.S. Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) after KrebsOnSecurity reported this week that a CISA contractor intentionally published AWS GovCloud keys and a vast trove of other agency secrets on a public GitHub account. The inquiry comes as CISA is still struggling to contain the breach and invalidate the leaked credentials.

items.title: Alleged Kimwolf Botmaster ‘Dort’ Arrested, Charged in U.S. and Canada
items.description: Canadian authorities on Wednesday arrested a 23-year-old Ottawa man on suspicion of building and operating Kimwolf, a fast spreading Internet-of-Things botnet that enslaved millions of devices for use in a series of massive distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks over the past six months. KrebsOnSecurity publicly named the suspect in February 2026 after the accused launched a volley of DDoS, doxing and swatting campaigns against this author and a security researcher. He now faces criminal hacking charges in both Canada and the United States.

items.title: CISA Admin Leaked AWS GovCloud Keys on Github
items.description: Until this past weekend, a contractor for the Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) maintained a public GitHub repository that exposed credentials to several highly privileged AWS GovCloud accounts and a large number of internal CISA systems. Security experts said the public archive included files detailing how CISA builds, tests and deploys software internally, and that it represents one of the most egregious government data leaks in recent history.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, May 2026 Edition
items.description: Artificial intelligence platforms may be just as susceptible to social engineering as human beings, but they are proving remarkably good at finding security vulnerabilities in human-made computer code. That reality is on full display this month with some of the more widely-used software makers -- including Apple, Google, Microsoft, Mozilla and Oracle -- fixing near record volumes of security bugs, and/or quickening the tempo of their patch releases.

items.title: Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
